title: Sealing draughts around roof lights and skylights
subtitle: Compressible strip, expanding foam, and reveal insulation for Velux-style openings
excerpt: Roof lights leak air at the frame-to-rafter junction, at the hinge seal, and through the kerb flashing. A compressible self-adhesive strip at the frame perimeter and flexible sealant at the flashing junction are a two-hour fix that stops the most common draught paths.
sourceNotes: Velux installation and maintenance guides (velux.co.uk, accessed May 2026). Energy Saving Trust draught-proofing guidance. NHBC Standards Chapter 7.2 on roof window installation.

glossaryTerms.definition: Self-adhesive foam, brush, or rubber strips applied to the meeting surfaces of doors, windows, lofts, and floor gaps to stop air infiltration. At roof lights, compressible EPDM strip applied to the frame-to-lining junction is the standard approach.

content.text: Roof lights in older loft rooms are a consistent draught source. The frame-to-lining gap where the roof light is built into the reveal plasterwork, the hinge mechanism, and the kerb flashing detail can all leak warm air to the cold roof void or directly to outside. A single roof light in a loft bedroom with a poor frame seal contributes noticeably to the room's heating demand.

On a cold day, close the roof light and hold a hand near the frame perimeter. The common locations: the gap between the frame and the timber reveal at each side, the gap at the head where the frame meets the plasterboard reveal, and the gap at the cill. Also check the hinge side: the hinge sash seal can compress unevenly, leaving a gap on one side when the window is closed.
Self-adhesive compressible EPDM foam strip, 6-10 mm wide x 4 mm thick (the standard roof light frame gap)
Flexible sealant: white or grey paintable flexible acrylic for the frame-to-plasterboard reveal gap
Clean the frame surface and the adjacent reveal surface with a dry cloth. Remove any old foam strip or sealant from previous attempts.
to the reveal face that meets the window frame when the window is opened: the strip is compressed by the frame when closed.

Apply flexible sealant to the visible gap between the window frame and the plasterboard reveal casing at the head and sides. Run a thin bead and smooth with a wet finger. This seals the gap in the lining rather than relying on the window closing to compress the seal.
Close the window and press the sash firmly against the frame to check the seal compresses evenly around the perimeter.

The sloping reveal around a roof light is often uninsulated plasterboard over a timber frame, with the cold structural timber of the rafter directly behind. The reveal surface is typically the coldest internal surface in the room. Fitting a 25 mm rigid foam panel between the reveal lining and the rafter substantially improves surface temperature and reduces condensation risk on the reveal. This requires opening the plasterboard reveal, which is a half-day job beyond the basic draught seal.

Roof light manufacturers supply replacement seal kits for their windows. On a 15-year-old or older roof light, the hinge seals are often compressed flat and no longer functional. A replacement seal kit (the EPDM gasket that runs round the sash) takes around 90 minutes to fit with a screwdriver and the manufacturer's instructions.
